preamble: Vowel deletion, specifically the loss of the close back rounded vowel /u/, is a common phonetic transformation known as apocope (word-final deletion) or syncope (word-medial deletion). This process often occurs in unstressed syllables, contributing to syllable reduction and changes in word structure.
phoneticEffects: Apocope, Syncope, Vowel Reduction, Syllable Reduction

examples.note: The final unstressed /u/ of the Proto-Germanic nominative singular ending *-uz was lost in the development to Old English.

sources: Versloot, A. P. (2014). Reduction of unstressed vowels in Proto-Frisian and the Germanic 'Auslautgesetze'. UvA-DARE (Digital Academic Repository).
Nishimura, K. (2010). Syncope in Latin: A historical restatement of its conditions. Academia.edu.
